questions.q: What is the primary purpose of SIFT (Scale-Invariant Feature Transform)?
options: To blur images for noise reduction
To detect and describe distinctive keypoints in images that are invariant to scale, rotation, and illumination changes
questions.explanation: SIFT identifies local features stable across image transformations. Keypoints are found at multi-scale extrema in Gaussian derivatives; descriptors encode local gradient information. This makes SIFT robust for matching and recognition.

questions.q: What is a keypoint in feature extraction?
options: A point marking the image center
A distinctive location (usually at corners or blobs) with high information content and repeatable detection under transformations
A random pixel selected for analysis
The brightest pixel in an image
questions.explanation: Keypoints are salient image locations—typically high-gradient regions like corners or blob centers—with distinctive neighborhoods suitable for matching. Repeatability across transformed views is essential.

questions.q: How does HOG (Histogram of Oriented Gradients) represent image features?
options: By computing histograms of gradient directions within local image patches, capturing edge structure and shape information
questions.explanation: HOG divides images into cells and computes gradient direction histograms per cell. This encodes shape and edge structure compactly, making it effective for object detection (e.g., pedestrians).

questions.q: What does feature matching accomplish in computer vision?
options: It finds correspondences between keypoints in two images using descriptor similarity, enabling alignment, 3D reconstruction, or scene understanding
questions.explanation: Feature matching compares descriptors of keypoints between images. Pairs with similar descriptors are likely the same point under different viewpoints, enabling image registration, panoramas, and structure-from-motion.

questions.q: Why is scale invariance important for feature descriptors like SIFT?
options: Objects appear at varying distances; scale-invariant descriptors detect the same feature across different image sizes and zoom levels
questions.explanation: Real-world objects change apparent size due to camera distance or zoom. Scale-invariant descriptors match features consistently across scales. SIFT uses Gaussian scale-space to achieve this.

questions.q: What is the purpose of computing descriptor vectors for keypoints?
options: To create compact, distinctive representations of local neighborhoods around keypoints, enabling robust matching between images
To record keypoint location coordinates only
questions.explanation: Descriptors encode local visual information around keypoints (e.g., SIFT's 128-D vector of gradient histograms). Similar descriptors in different images suggest corresponding scene points, enabling geometric verification and matching.

questions.q: How do ORB (Oriented FAST and Rotated BRIEF) features differ from SIFT in computational efficiency?
options: ORB uses faster corner detection (FAST) and binary descriptors (BRIEF) instead of Gaussian scale-space and 128-D vectors, making it suitable for real-time applications
questions.explanation: SIFT is computationally expensive due to scale-space construction and 128-D descriptors. ORB accelerates via FAST corner detection and binary descriptors, enabling mobile and embedded vision at the cost of slight accuracy loss.

questions.q: What role do local maxima in scale-space play in SIFT keypoint detection?
options: Keypoints are located at extrema (maxima/minima) in Difference-of-Gaussian scale-space, indicating distinctive multi-scale features
questions.explanation: SIFT builds Gaussian pyramids and computes Difference-of-Gaussians (DoG) across scales. Local extrema in 3D DoG space (spatial + scale dimensions) yield scale-invariant, stable keypoints.

questions.q: How does feature extraction enable image retrieval?
options: Features from a query image are matched against features in a database; similar features across images enable ranking and retrieval of visually similar images
It only works for text-based search
questions.explanation: Image retrieval systems extract features from query and database images, compute descriptor similarities, and rank database images by match quality. This enables content-based search without semantic labels.

questions.q: What is the significance of orientation assignment in SIFT descriptors?
options: It is optional and often ignored
Assigning dominant gradient orientation to keypoints enables rotation invariance; descriptors computed relative to this orientation match under rotations
Orientation has no impact on matching
questions.explanation: SIFT assigns each keypoint a dominant local gradient direction. Descriptors are computed in this rotated coordinate frame, ensuring that rotated views of the same scene produce identical descriptors.
